speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: This is Axel Anderson, Interview Number 4.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I believe it was in 50. Yeah, in 50. 1950.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I put in two days and two nights on that and I never went to bed.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was a crew, we had camp 36 up there on the Blue's, oh, 5 or 6 miles above Laird's camp, you know, up there in the pocket. So we had that crew on it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was, no, wait a minute, now I'm, I'm, I'm... It was when the, oh no, 1950, that was in the, during the war days, they had 1200 sailors on it from the camp out of Cardlin.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What do you call that? What do you call that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know the name of the one. No, no, that's here, it's okay.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What do they call that camp now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I guess it's named it. Been there several times since, too.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they had 1200 sailors there and they, they, they couldn't do anything. The captain was sitting down in the valley there at the store at the, down in the meadows. And they had a walkie -talkie, you know, you could talk to them. So the wind come up and flared up a little bit, they had to retreat. He called them back and, well, I remember what little they had done in the day, you know, they lost that. And it cooled off a little bit and they go back and make a little more trail and because they get another wind, puff come and away they go again down. So they didn't get anything done. We got in there with a couple of trees of ushers and we had a corral in the afternoon. With that little group we had about 70, oh 70, 80 men. And we had four dozers. Corral in a little bit. Then we backfired. We made, they call it the Saunders fire.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, it burnt, yeah, old side it burnt, you know. It burnt a big area. Oh that burnt a hell of a big area.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Maybe there was 40, could have been 44 then maybe, they were doing the war anyway.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how that fire started?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I guess lightning had most.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When you came back after World War I, when you came back to the woods, did you start in as walking boss right away?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I don't camped after that. No, by God yes, I started in, I don't camp for seven months in the spring or the summer. No, I got the walking boss in after this Spokane fair in September. Back in March, you see.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What did that mean when you became walking boss?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you go, I got charge of everything that belonged to the woods department, you see. I was assistant superintendent, I had a man over me. And I was roadmaster, I looked after all the railroad tracks. Train master, I looked after all the trains and whatever have you. And also went to eat from one camp to the other. I had to go to the camp with, oh I saw each one of them at least once a week. First one camp and then the other. But then I had all kinds of duties between that. They always needed something, you know.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was that just for the Elk River area?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was for the Elk River Mill then, you see. The mill was running there then. That ran up to 1930, the last.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: To December the 22nd, 1930, that was the end of it. And it started in 1911. They built it in 10, started the next year. She ran that many years. They built it I guess for 20 years, operation. And then, oh I don't know why, that was what you never know. It would have been timber there yet, they could have had that mill running all the time and still running if they wanted to. They're still logging there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then hauling the timber over here to Newport and I guess most of it goes to Newport and Cusick on the boundary river. The Diamond National still logs up there and haul it over. And Potlatte too, I think all the past Elk River and down to Potlatte.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Well, was your job to make sure that the foremen were doing the right work? Or was it to help the foremen out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Help the foremen if I could, yeah. Yeah, that was my job.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And we all had to work together. It all had to work together, you see, just like any other team. We had the railroad to transfer to get the logs away from them. Well, if we didn't work together and have a train there and get the logs loaded and take them away from them, the foremen couldn't do nothing. It all had to work together as a team.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And as yet, they had to do the same thing now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And for supply and so forth, you know, that more or less had to see that the roads were open and equipment to bring it into them. They were miles away from no place. So every one or each one needed a other. I needed their help and they needed mine.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's it, yes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: From the middle out.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you go back home every night?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or would you... I did at Elk River. I walked in. I usually come home about 10, 11 o 'clock at night. I left at 5 in the morning.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you sleep with your boots on too?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, most of the time I took my shoes off. But a lot of times that's the only thing I had off from Sunday night till Saturday evening. The only thing I took off was the shoes and the hat. I lay down on top of the bunk in the cab somewhere and slept a couple of hours.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Gooey.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: After we got the trucks and all that, one summer there we had... That's up on the tree there up at Mason Meadow. We had 18 log trucks and four pole trucks. Two loading crews in the... double crew. There were three loading crews in the woods. They were at the camp. Each one had six trucks to load. A 20 mile haul down to the railroad head. And a double crew, them two said there was six loading crews every day in the woods and two at the railroad. Two more at the railroad load them on flat cars. And they went to work at 4 o 'clock in the morning and first crew worked eight hours up to noon and the other crew come and took over and load to 8 o 'clock at night. 16 hours. And I was on my feet all the way, all the way there. And then after that they were going and done with that. You know, see that they was running smooth all day. And I'd go down and see how the train crews were getting on the railroad track course. There were 50 miles of railroads from there into Bowville that we hauled every day on the rails.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had two camps there. One up in the woods and one at the railroad.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Six section crews on the road between there and Bowville. So I had to be able to, in the morning when I got out, I had to be able to stand there at the camp and look from there to Bowville, 50 miles, and see where the crews was. I had a phone along the road but that was all. I could talk, you know, so they could talk to me too once in a while. The train crew had to use it, you know, for one course there was two or three train crews on the road. Then we had to keep them apart so they don't run into each other.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was fun, a lot of fun. It kept me busy. I was busy from morning to night. This one sounds like a lot of work to me. The day was never long enough. The day was always too short and it's only 24 hours.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So most of the time I worked for 24 hours, a lot of times.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you walk that whole length on a lot of those days?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I had a little speeder on the rail. Most of the time that I used. Little bitty ones that I could pick up and take it off, set it off. I knew where the train was. I heard them coming, I seen them coming. I'd pick it off, set it on track and they go and I go the other way. I met another one. I could go 40 miles an hour on the rail. It didn't take long to go. But an hour I could go from one end to the other, 50 miles.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It took a little over an hour to find a clear track. Didn't get off the track.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Won't shut down.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was that all one camp? Was that called?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had one camp that time.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Which camp was it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was 30... let me see, 34 I think we called it. 32 and 34. One on the end of the rail and one on the end of the chalk road.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, two camps that summer.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's the only one we had for potlats.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Mail.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They only run one train tonight. We got 32 carloads every day.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But how much wood would that be?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, that was around, oh, anyway, 220 to 240 thousand feet, a log scale.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Quarter of a million.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But in there a day we got...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Two shifts, you know, then I used to have to get between 50 and 60 carloads when they run double shifts.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then we had five, six logging camps, but after the depression had cut down, they were more fed. They got more... They didn't waste so much in the mill. They got more particularly after that. Saved the lumber more. They got more boat feed out of the logs than they did before.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They utilized the timber better, saved more of it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They even saved the sawdust. Now they saved the bark too.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did they just burn an area after they locked it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, God, yes, you bet you burned it. You bet you set fire to it in every spring and burnt it. Stay away from the hazard in the summer when it got dry. Yes, you used to burn that every spring, burn the sledge ring.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then later years then they piled it and burnt it in the fall. Piled it first. But before that they just burned... Just broadcast it, just let it go.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did it get away often?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, not too much, no.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, yeah, they took the old crew and they usually... Usually they had it drenched off and... The sledge ring was pretty much in one area so you could... ...tranch it first before you set fire and they set from the outside and let it work in itself.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Have it all drenched up and start fire on the way the... ...if the fire was go...if the wind was from the north, you know, burn a good strip on the south side first. Then go over on the north side and set it and now we're in the cross. But you had protection then, you see, they had it burnt out so the spike wouldn't fly. And then they had a big crew there towards it if anything should happen to come across, you know, they were right there to stop.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Kill it out.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, you burnt a lot of sledge ring there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sections of it every spring, just as soon as the snow get off, got off the ground, you know, then it's dry enough. And some evening when you thought it might rain that night, set fire to it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did they then reseed it all? Did they?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Most of it. Re -seeded itself naturally. Usually at that ground it's full of seeds, you know. So if a quick fire goes over, well there's lots of it, then that gives the seed a chance to come up again. If it's like in the summer, when a fire goes out it burns too deep. It burns all that needles and moss and stuff, you know, where the seed is laying. That burns that up and burns too deep, you know, there is no seed left. But like we burnt in the spring, you know, a couple of years then it was green like a wheat field.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sometimes, you know, they come so damn thick, you know, when you stepped there you probably stepped on a couple of dozen young seedlings. They were just like a mat come up.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then it dies out itself. Damn, years now I see they turn it out, prune the trees and turn out. That'll make the roof lot faster, but in the early years they didn't do that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know some places you can see along the road, you know, where they've been in and they prune the trees, you know, up as high as they can reach. And it looked like a park in there, you can see a long ways.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Haven't you noticed that? Along the highway, around the road when you go up in the upper country, yeah?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That looked pretty nice. Down towards Clarkia and down that way. I see they do that quite a bit, or they used to do it. When they had the CCs in there, they turned the light over there down around Clarkia towards Bowville. And that looked pretty nice.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I see they do it now more than the guys said they ever did.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do with the forest service in that area?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, not very. Oh no, just two fires and so forth, that is all. Only I worked with them during the 3C outwits. I was six months up here on the Pound Ray River and six months up on the St. Joe up at Herrick. I worked for them a year there, during the hard years there in 33, 33 it was, 33 and 34.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But when you were working around Elk River, they didn't ever complain about something that you were doing?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was not too much forest land in there. There was a state and we had the state fire patrol in there. Potlat's Forest, Potlat's Protective, they got their office in Northie, no one in North, they used to have a station at Elk River too. They have their own fire department in North Potlat, yes. And he is also a state fire warden. So they had to take care of the state. And the forest service come in there a little bit, not too much. They come in there with the Blister Rust and had a few camps in there. But we never had too much set with the forest service until that first fire I was on there was on that Saunders fire, that was the forest service fire. And they come and got us.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: So they used to be there but they didn't used to have very much to do with most of the woods around the country?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Only up when we logged up there at the Avery. Half of it, there was some of it was anpilla and free land, you know that they got when they built the roads. They got every other section, forest service had one and NP had the other. That's what they logged there for several years. Well they're still logging now, I guess part of it I think now. I mean for the last 30 years now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then they had a little, when they logged on, I don't remember now, I think NP had number one section and forest service they evened too. See that's the one, one two you know. So like the NP had one, three, five, seven and forest service had two, four, six and so on. And that was that whole damn country there was oh dear I don't know there was hundreds of sections there. All them mountains belonged to the NP and the forest service. Then you come in a little contact with them. Now I see they got, when you go in on the forest service land you know they you got to build a road according to where the forest service one of them. They stick them out for you and you got to build them to their specification I guess. When I was there you know we just went in and built them.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Well did you log on the land that was forest service owned or was it, it was forest service land that you logged on?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes indeed.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They paid them the stumpage you know, paid that to the government. Whatever they paid for that owned. And the same the other belonged to the NP railroad. So they paid them, scaled it and paid them so much stumpage. Whatever that was I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Go and sit down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's it. Good dog.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh he likes to get around to a little bit. No to.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Gabe?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'll give you a prune. Bag that there good. You pick one off on the tree.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got the stumpage too much.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: A bullet of woods. A bullet of woods. Ha ha ha.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Ha ha ha.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have very much going on when you first started as walking boss?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Was there a, because it seems like a big jump to have taken to you know be in charge like that. There was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When you started out you didn't know what the hell you were looking for but you didn't know what you run into before the day was out.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When you started out in the morning you had to be able to, when I worked on Boville I had six camps and a steel gang. That was the seventh.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I had six train crews and a loading crew with each one of them. And I had a section crew at each camp. That was my job to see to look after all them train crews after all them loading crews. Look after all the reloading crews at each camp. That was part of it. And then in the meantime you know go and look up the camp boss and see what he needed and wanted. So I started out Monday morning and usually I come back to go Saturday afternoon. One last something broke down I had to go to the shop you know and that was usually the only night. We had a shop out of Boville. Every Sunday we brought in two or three and two usually two loading rigs and two locomotives and three of them every Sunday and rotated them back and forth. So every Sunday I had to shop. Sunday was just the best day of any other day. See that they got fixed up for the week. See them locomotives had to wash them. That water is not too good. We got the water from them trees, you know out of the creek where we could siphon it up in the tank. Mud and lots of alkali in it. They get corroded, the boilers get corroded on the inside and you had to wash that out you know about every two weeks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had to wash them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Clean them boilers. Then towards the end of two weeks you know they start foaming. They got too much mud inside of that you see. That would work very good. Just like soap and the crudes you know and you had to get that off. Inside I had them clean. So yeah I had seven days a week and that many nights. Never missed. They all come.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that when you started out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh I done camped for a long time. I done camped for over ten years there. I started out on the crew in Lebanon. Part of the time I was in France. I was there three years after that. 17, 18 and 19. Part of 19.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were there many guys Axel that were working seven days and seven nights a week?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Everybody was on the monthly payroll. You got paid by the month and you were sold. You were sold to the company.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You were on 24 hour duty.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You had to expect you to look after what you were supposed to do. A lot of it you know things broke down. You had to get it fixed. Break down in the afternoon something. You had to get it fixed and get it out and go to work in the morning again. Somebody had to look after it. And the one in charge of it he used to usually the one that was hooked and going to get. Because you paid the crews that were working under you. They were paid by the day you know the night or whatever when you call them out you know they were paid. But the monthly man he was the one that was hooked.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There were no day out for no vacation time and no overtime. You were paid by the month. So much a month and that was it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now you know I think well later I think they will take half of this and other. They will take half of this and other now to do what I did.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or more.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: What I did 40 years ago.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now I see they know they had they find just before I left the working they had a foreman for every 8, 10 or 12 men. And I had 200, 300.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were twice their own camp you know. We only had one camp. Because then I had a lot of assistant. I had three assistant. Had a cat boss.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had a big recording over at the site. They had to be on the rail that was moving.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they had to be serviced at night. Greased and fuel and cleaned up. If they were broke down they would get parts to them and fix them up. So they would go in the morning again.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They had a big house. That was a big item right there. To feed. There in one camp there was 350 men. In one camp there was a little town.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had hot water running water in the bunk cars and a bath house and kitchen everywhere.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They had a lot of food. That kitchen stove was never cooled off. From spring to the next one. The year round that stove was hot. Took a lot of wood.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What did you get paid a month when you started as walking boss?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: $250 a month.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See the wages didn't come up until about the time I quit. That's when the wages came up. After 50. I think I got 600. And bored. They didn't charge me for that. Because I was eating in every place. I had a charge account. If I was in town I would just turn it in. Wherever I stayed. In the hotel. I couldn't always get to where I wanted to go. So I stopped in the Mary's or Moscow or Phoenix or Cardilene. Wherever I was I would go to the hotel and at the end of the month turn it in for meals and lodging. Meats and lodging and car fare if I used to train.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: A little book marked down each day whatever I spent. At the end of the month turn it in. And I get my money back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now I imagine probably they are there now probably pay $1500 a month.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: 3 or 4 times as much as when I was working.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they don't have, there is no ATR days now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they have so many assistance. I see now that the Logan superintendent over there at Boville he got ATR 10 walking around doing nothing just under him.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the same with the camp foreman. He's the foreman in the camp and he's got for every 9 men he has an assistant.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Two unions and all of that stuff. I think that's over done. Everybody thinks they're doing so damn much. I can hear people now there's young fellas and oh they're so tired. And they've been sitting on the drive in a car all day. They're so tired. I never got tired until I was hurt so bad that I couldn't hardly walk and I was still working. But you know then I knew what tired was. I got to after I got hurt there you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So they later to the years there I knew what it is to get tired. But later I got up in the 60's I never felt tired. Got a cup of coffee and a donut and I was good going on 8 hours. Nothing to it. No sleep or anything. That didn't bother me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now the young fellas they get so damn tired they say if they drive 4 or 5 hours along the road sitting in the car oh my god I'm just all in. I get so tired. I can drive from here to Frisco and I don't get tired yet. Never hurt me any. Only that I got to slow down now in the evening. I don't want to be out on the road after dark. That bothers me driving otherwise that wouldn't bother me. I'm going down there next month I'm going to take a little trip to Oregon and California. Maybe go down as far as Frisco.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: If you'd wanted to just work 5 or 6 days a week could you have done it if you'd wanted to? Now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Back then did you have any choice about how hard and how long you worked?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. No there was daylight to dark. There was no hours then. They expected daylight to dark. There was supposed to be 10 hours but in the winter time it was daylight to dark.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And there was no hours. When I first started to keep time there, first run crew, if they were out a little bit in the morning you always give them a quarter of a day. 2 hours and a half or something. If they were a little over half of that time then you put in a half a day. Or if they were out working a little while after dinner you give them a full day. That's the way we do understand. Now it's by the hour. Everything is by the hour. All they think about now is to get the hour in and they don't care much about the work. If they do anything or not, as long as they get the hour.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: All the cook -outs, crews, they were all working by the day. They get so much a month. Cook and now I think everything is hourly work. See anything you work past these hours there's got to be a time and a half. And Sunday double.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When you were walking boss, what kind of amount of territory would you cover in a day? Where would you go, let's say if you started out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well I tried to make a system out of it. See now when I worked on Boville we had a camp over toward Clark. That was the one on the Moscow mountain down in from Princeton. And we had one up the Plews River or Harvard. And we had one on Bear Creek out of Avon. And we had two of them in from Helmer. And now you see I tried to make out a system. So I don't start maybe I run up towards Clark here and on Monday was in Boville there and see that the train crews get out Sunday evening to each camp where they belong. So they were ready for Monday morning summer. I had to go to clean down there to Moscow mountain. Go down to around to Princeton and then go eat about 10 -15 miles up on the mountain. And one or two more, one out of Avon or Derry up Bear Creek. They had to be out there. They couldn't leave Boville and see and go to work. They had to be there in the morning and go to work. So we had them out Sunday afternoon. They come in Saturday evening and go out Sunday afternoon. The same with the one go at Clark here, train crew and so forth. You know well I had to do the same thing. So I figured on leaving Boville Monday morning and I had to go with one camper there on the stay overnight and that one next day go to next. Look over see what they were doing. Next night I was in the third camp and so forth. Next night in another one. Long as towards Saturday afternoon I worked my way up around Boville. I lived in that area.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You'd spend a whole day in a camp often?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh God yes you know there's a lot of territory in that camp. It covered six, seven sections.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Oh so you'd go out and check out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Just see what they were doing. Just mosey around checking. Having the phone fine was fine. Me and the boss walking around he showed me what they were doing fine. Anything needed I'd get it for him. He'd have it the next day. When the supply wagon come around with it. We had a phone in the camp you know and I could call in every night. Go on to the warehouse and tell them what they needed. Next day they would come out. We had a hotel team go in from the camp you know and from each camp horses. They load them up with what they needed. So at night they'd have it back there again. Yes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That kept me busy. I wasn't hiding a minute.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was it that they wanted to have someone else to check out the camp that they had to go around?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well they only had one. They didn't want to pay anymore.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I meant each of these camps had a camp warning. Yes.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And then they wanted someone to go out and check for the camp for them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sure sure. Go vet them. Go vet them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know when you do anything you know two heads sometimes are better than one. Unless you think so.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Lots of times you know the foreman had something that he was doubtful about. He'd have to have it or he'd say go ahead and do it if it's alright. And if it wasn't alright you know we'd talk it over and then maybe I had a better suggestion than he did or maybe his was better than mine so he agreed to go ahead.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know you take the camp usually had around 150 men to the camp ten days for each camp. And there were six of them. Well you know the foreman there you know he paid them even though that rate is only two dollars or two and a quarter, two and a half. It's a lot of money.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And you got to have a little work out of them.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you help the foreman work out getting chances and that sort of thing?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes yes. He'd go over and look over and see where he'd build a road. If you should have a railroad up there I built them for them then I built them. I'd get the upper crews up there. Build that road and get the steel on and have the track car and then it was up to him after I'd get the logs to it. A lot of places you know they probably couldn't get there so they'd build a railroad you know where they could go with the horses and get it. Other places you know probably put a donkey in and got it. Things like that you know that come up every day first going camping and the other.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was yet I think. Because now they got an engineer partly at TAS and he's got a crew and he's staking roads all the time you know for truck roads. Then the damn days you know we had to do it ourselves. Had nobody. Us take out miles, hundreds of miles of railroads. We had nobody then you know for god's sake. There's a 2 or 3 X man with me and we'd take out a couple of miles of railroads in a couple or 3 hours as fast as we could go. We had an ebony level you know we would side it up and go on percentage you know 5 % or whatever it took. If it was steeper than that we'd be a lot of road on 10 % go up the side and line it up, make a kickbacks and loop the loops. Follow the contour of the ground and build it by hand.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Pick and treble.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's before we had the dosers. The dosers didn't come in until the 30's you see. And the later part of the 30's the railroad went out so we didn't use the... We had steam shovels a little bit. We could use the last few years in the 20's. But there were no dosers. They didn't come until the 30's.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So in the early days everything was done by hand.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Strong back and weakened the top.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any special way that you learned to lay out those railroads or was it just plotting it out by the contour of the land?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's it. Best, easiest, quickest way we could get there.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Could you just sight it as you went along? Just keep moving? Keep walking along?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: you had to have a certain...you know... You got to have the steepest we could go in the curvature was 24 degrees. That's about as much you could bend the rail and keep any machinery on the rail. You had to watch for that. When you come with the train you know you got to have a little piece of tension so the car come out of one curve so they can level up again before they hit the next one. Otherwise they get off the rail. Oh no, you had to figure. You had to know what you were doing. I tell you now, there wasn't nobody...there wasn't that thrown in by just by guessing and be damned. No, no, you had to know what you were doing. Or else you couldn't...they wouldn't stay on the rail, them cars.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But alright, good luck.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now we had this rail up by three double sweats back going up on Elk Butte. But we went up there on 7 % grade. They take four loaded cars that is already there to have gone to one locomotive. 7 % that's pretty steep you know. 7 feet up to 100 if anything should happen you know. We had a lot of runs. But you know around the side hill you're going up on 7 this way till you hit the river or farther. Then come back just enough for a tail end so you have room for a 5 -6 car and a locomotive. Then go the other way again as far as you can. Then keep going, switch back, what we call them. I had three of them going up there to a four to go up on Elk Butte, both sweats back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You see you start out and go up as far as you can then turn around and go the other way as far as you can. Back up, then go again the other. Just reverse you know, keep on going up the same side till you get up where you wanted to go. You got up to the top of it. And where they took the locomotive almost up on top of Elk Butte.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's why I could stay downtown you know, even down in Elk Haven and hear them trees going and see them. Going up the side hill there you know, see the smoke going up and hear them. And you knew where they were.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Going up in the upper basin there up Elk Creek itself. We made it just like a horseshoe. We come up the creek you know, and here was a fork of it. We come up with a 5 percent grade. And here was one brown going on, the other one went there. When we come on up here across the creek and come on over, made a loop here, come in here again and turn around across this one. And come back and go on up that one up to the end. It was just like a horseshoe. When you come down with the locomotive you know, the biggest train we took down there was 42 loaded cars. We had 420 ,000 feet of saw logs behind that little locomotive. 80 ton little rod engine, 3 wheeler. Go down there with it. I was on the engine here and I could see the tail end up here. That was up about 50 feet higher and there was that above us. And there were 42 cars between that caboose and the locomotive. They were going around the turn. But we put in there, met the caboose. But that was better than a switchback. We didn't have room enough to switch back and you had to gain that much. There were so many hundred feet of elevation you had to get up that high. And you had to try to get that much distance to get up there. To make that down there. So we stayed under 5 percent on a sharp curve and that was down to 3. Because you know you can't trip or pull so many on a curve that's good on a straight deck. We took 21 empties up that way. And we took twice that many loads going down.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would shades ever get away?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, they run away. I picked up a lot of them. They were all laying in a heap up against the side hill somewhere. Or in the creek. Don't pick them up. Take them in. Fix them up again. Next day we had them out. A few days and they were gone again.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How fast would they go?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh you could get up to 12, about 10 -12 miles an hour. They were geared locomotive. They had what they call a line shaft and a pinion gear on each. Just like a pinion gear in a car.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And that turned for each one of them wheels. Every wheel had power on it. They were all pulling. There was 6 double trucks under them. And there was a water tank and the whole work and everything had power underneath of them. Three cylinders on them shades. They were up and down on the side.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then we had another type they called the climax.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Them two pistons, they come into the drive gear right under the boiler, right in the middle. Them two pistons, they went up and down this way.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And then they had another one, what the hell did they call that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Them were laying like this in front on the outside. And then the simple one, because you see them here straight. But the geared one, they were either come this way down, worked on that pinion underneath there, you know. From both sides, and they say when the others laid like this in the drive shaft. And they worked like that on the pinion underneath.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were the guys that did a shade cruise, were those guys as good at doing shades as say the guys were on the regular railroads?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, every bit of it. You bet they had to know the air and the steam.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they watch it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You bet they did. There were a lot of difference in men, you know. Some could do pretty damn good and others didn't have quite the knack to get the work out of them. Some like a driving car, you know, you see there's a lot of difference in drivers on the car. Some is just perfect and the others, you know, they just nip and took. They get there, but that's... Yes, you bet there was a lot of difference. And the air, you know, they didn't know how much they could draw off a few pounds, you know, because they had them air pumps on there. And you had... Sometimes you had to draw it down quite a little bit and see that everything was working so they didn't stop on you when you take. When we started down there from the... I took a lot of them Milwaukee locomotive engineers up with us. By God, they got off and walked. They weren't there to write down when we went down on them. They were, well, you know, they have a 1 % and we were running on 10. And, you know, you take that little machine that we had, you know, and you see them flat cords up there 200 feet higher than you were. That's a lot of pressure, a lot of weight there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When you see them up coming down the side there, they won't try it. Oh no, they said too much for me. I walk. I'll catch you at the bottom. We probably had a couple of switchbacks to go through and you can go straight down and walk down and catch them in the bottom.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How many men were on a Che crew?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Like the firemen and the engineer. Conductor and the brakemen, they were four. Sometimes five. If they had long trains, they had put two brakemen. But the U .S. were loading, they had four men crew. Two on the machine and two on the ground to do the coupling and set the end brakes and do the search.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was the best job in the whole damn woods. The train crew, they got paid from the time they left camp till they come back. So they made good money.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They made more than I did.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did they work hard all the time that they were out or did they have more slack time? The train crew?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, they sat down and loaded. They probably sat down there about there for ten hours.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Ever moved?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sit.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Won't go out there and do a little flagging now and then. And the firemen, they were sitting in the machine, you know, just back up a little bit to come ahead a little bit and so on, where they were loading. But they had to be there. They were part of it. No, that was the best job in the woods was the train crews. They sometimes get, they worked by the hour. They got paid by the hour. They worked for you. They would have no monthly on them because they, sometimes they only had a, got a day. They wait until the next, the next time, you know, they probably worked fifteen, seventeen hours. And they got paid so much an hour. That was the best job in the woods. I never got on to that. I could have got on to the Milwaukee. And I thought I got a hundred dollars a month on board, you know, by God, I thought that was better. Because the railroad wasn't as much like it is now then either. I don't know what they did, but I made more money than a hundred dollars on board, you know. And that was, I thought was something. But I could have got on there and in a few years I'd have been the conductor. I was talking to one of them, got on after I, after I could have got on when they had the N .I .R .E. And he worked twenty -one days that month and he had nine hundred dollars. He worked for the Milwaukee conductor. And I worked thirty -one days and I got a hundred and forty. And I worked thirty -one days and partly nights. Most of the summer a lot of nights too. And I only got a hundred and forty dollars. Twenty years later.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So you see, I made a lot of foolish moves that I didn't take.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, there must have been some good things about being walking, bossy. You wouldn't have done it for so long. Well, that's what you see. Well, you get tied up in it and you, after you get a family and start raising a family, you know, you can't jump the fence like you can when you're single.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And like on the rail, to get back on the railroad, there is a time when you're, it's always a time in your life, in all of us, for all of us. If you could see it and take it when that time is there. But if you miss that boat, well then you're out. You have to go on with what you started.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Stay with it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I did. I missed that boat. I didn't take it. But I thought there was a future in the woods and it didn't happen.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The machine aids come in and the machine, you know, that's up now. You take the conductor on the rails now, he's doing. They got to run, you know, like most of them there I saw talk to some of them that was starting in on the rail when the Milwaukee first come out there. I remember when I was living down there in Spokane, now Downey, Jack Downey. I've known them since 1911. He lived down there in Spokane. And here the last time I saw him when he was working, he had to run from Seattle to

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, Mowbridge.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That's in South Dakota, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: On the fast train. He go down this way and then he go back again and make it two trips a week or something like that, you know, and he'd have. But you, probably there weren't many guys that had the kind of responsibility that you did. Maybe you didn't make the money, but you sure had the responsibility. Well, that's how I got to work, yes. I had plenty of work. I agree with you there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But according to now, there is no comparison with it now when it was then.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It changed so much, you know that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What did your wife think that you were out so much and worked so hard all the time?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Was that... She worked like hell too. We had sick kids. She worked like hell too and had very much time to think about anything else. Only take care of the kids.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, there wasn't... there is no comparison now than what it was 40 years ago.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did your job change very much in the 20s? Did logging the way they logged change very much?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, the only way I can explain it to you is you got a new ground to work on every day. The ground tomorrow isn't like it was today. You're on new ground all together. See, when you cut the trees down like from... you cut this lot here, today, but tomorrow, you got to be over there and that's different. Everything is new to you. You got started again. You're starting new every morning in the woods.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You ain't building like when you build a sawmill. They build that, you know, running for 50 years. Well, they won't think it's just tomorrow. It's just like it was today. You see that the machines are running. They're sitting on the same place. But when you're out in the timber, you're on new country every morning. New ground all together.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And you got to go like a look ahead. You got to go up there in the timber and look ahead so you know that today, you're all right there. Now tomorrow, we build that road up there a little bit and you're there all right. You go over in that direction, it's the same thing. But you got to go and look up ahead, you know, when you build a road. So you might walk up there five miles to see. Someday you got to get there and when you start to build a road in the bottom, can't build it that one day. You're working, working your way up there. And you got to figure the percentage that's against you. And having that figured out so by the time you get that road up to the top, you got to get them logs too. So you got to go and look a hell of a lot ahead. For miles and miles ahead. And that was mostly my job to do, you know, when I got to the camp, you know. See, because I built the road for most of them so they got up to the top of the hills. And we had rails them days, you know, and that had to be figured out ahead. So I had to work, my work was mostly miles ahead of the camp for me in the timber. If I couldn't do it today, next week I come back then I run up there again and I put in two, three, four hours. And I come down, down to camp, you know, just to go, chopper time, a little after. And then probably I had 15 miles to walk back home. Had to be downtown in the morning and see, get them train crews out again. See that they were all set. So I walked home every night, you know, 15, 20 miles. When I was still working, we worked longer hours than any other in the camp. And then I head to town in the morning, I was out at five o 'clock and they left. They usually left town, the train crews, we tied them all in town there at that river first. And get them out so they were up in the camp and go to work at seven o 'clock.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I just can't believe how hard that must have been. I mean it just seems like the amount of walking and the amount of work you did was just more than a man could do.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm here.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I know, and you're in good health too. I'm still here. Starting in on the 89th year. But I can feel it. I can feel the age now. I'm always thinking about that, what Alan Ludden said. When you get into the 70s, you're pretty well at the end of the rope. Tie a knot in it and hang on. And I've been hanging on for quite a while. Still hanging on.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I wouldn't forget that. It was a pretty good reminder.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you run into situations where you thought the foreman wasn't doing a good job?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, yes. Sure thing. That happened.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Most of the time you say, try another one. Tie a cantoom and say, try another one. Oh yes, that can happen.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you fire him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, yes I'd fire him.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I never fired anybody in the camp. I only fired the bondy work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I always fired them when they were out working. Anybody. I never fired anybody in the camp. In the morning, a lot of foremen, they go out and pick them in the morning. The clerk wants to see you.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You got a partner?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Take them with you. The clerk wants to see you. We didn't say, that was a fish act.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I'd always, if I fired anybody, it would be when they were working. The clerk had someone working. If they were flacking, I'd tell them, you weren't even trying to work with me. So go on in and get the hell out of here. That's the only thing you can do. But do it on the job. Where everybody see it. And you do that a few times and you don't have much trouble after that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They all work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I never picked out anybody and when they come out in the morning and go to work, I let them go. And go to work, sure. I get them out on the job and doing nothing, okay. You don't want to work, so I ain't going to hold you here. You're going into camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was my way of doing it. Would you watch pretty carefully before you decided or would you just catch a guy and then say... I had a, what they call, you know, when you come in and they work the turn in at night and you see what you're... And you see, you can watch the scale report. See how many logs you got on this one and what you got on that one.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Everywhere.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So you got your men and go and talk to them the next day and what's the matter with you. You don't get the saw logs.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What's wrong here?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Someone will just lay down, you know, and don't do it. You don't care to work, so I can't hold you. I got to have saw logs. We got to have saw logs. We got to keep that saw mill going and by God, we can't do it with the likes of you. So I got to get somebody else here to take you here.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's about the only way you can handle it. You see at night, you see how many saw logs you got in when the scale report come in. And you can look at the scale sheet, you know, for each crew. Either it was a donkey or a team or whatever it was. You watch each one of them after and see where there was something. There was something wrong and you go up there the next day and see what was wrong.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because there had to be something. Maybe I could help them. Maybe it was the crew if everything was okay so you can see. That crew was just gold breaking. They weren't doing anything. They were just trying to get the day in and that's all. So you might as well get rid of them and try somebody else. But you do that a few times, you know, and you wouldn't have to pick up the... It would last a few days and that's all. Would you ever hire guys back after... Oh yes, oh yes, oh yes, maybe the next morning. Sure, you bet. Oh no, you can't carry no grudge against them, no.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh no.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Hell yes, you know, meet them in town and if it's still out of your job, well, come on back. Come on and go to work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sure.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no, that wouldn't work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Wife used to tell me one time to come up to the house, you know, somebody come over and we'd argue there. And almost come to fist and a little bit we'd talk it over. She says, I can't see it, how in the hell can you sit there and just talk to her one minute, you're ready to fight. Next minute she says, then talk like you're a better two gold friend. Well, sure. Trashed it out, had it out, leveled it all up again. Now he's all right and I feel better. And he's going to work again. And he feel good about it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So did I.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Heck yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now I've been told about once or twice when you got cursed out by a guy that you fired. And they said you didn't care. No, no way.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What's the use?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, because you meet them every day. That was my job, to meet them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Hell yes, you know. I had so many names that it is no book. Ain't all in the book.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Once you became walking boss, would you still fire crews on the job or was that up to the foreman to do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that depends on. I never went to camp because that was his job. You could do something that you didn't like and you could call them and say, I saw so and so doing so and so. I don't think it's right. That's up to you now to straighten that out. I don't like to fan it again. Oh no, you go to the foreman and that's his duty. But I had too many on my own. I had hundreds on him. I had more than he did. On my own he was carrying over the whole place. Over a hundred mile circuit. He only had eight or ten.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that men worked better under some foreman than others or did it not matter?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, I think that happened. Yes, I believe so. I think it was yet. I had a few things. You know, there is no man that can suit everybody. That's impossible. He isn't born yet. Never will be. No, you take what's in that operation. We had eight hundred men, I guess, scattered around. Or nine. Up to a thousand men, you see, scattered all over the woods. Well, you know, no matter who it is, you're a good fella on this side and you're a son of a bitch on that side and that's it. One thinks you're all right and the next one says, oh that son of a bitch, he is absolutely no good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's the way the foreman put up with you. You can't, no matter how you try, you know, you can't, no matter how honest and level and square you try to be, you know, you feel that somebody think you're not doing the right thing with him.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Personally.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I kept time. Run the crew and a lot, you know, someone, you couldn't raise the wages. They had to set from the main office, you know, to set the wages. And you liked the men and you would like to do something for them. And you were so asleep in the day that he didn't work, he might have had nothing that morning. So when the time broke, you know, I'd back in the day from just the same and he was laying in the bunk. Well, at the end of the month of the pay, they come, you know, sometimes you got by with it for a long time.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: After a while, you know, they start to compare the checks and they went around the head and then they come, you know, how did you get so much more than I did? How did you get a day more than I did?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You were even living camp today and by God, you got your check is same as mine or maybe more. So there you are. Then the boss is in hot water. He comes to you and asks why the elderly get so much more than I did.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So you can't be, no matter what you do, sometimes you do the wrong thing. You're trying to do the right thing and you're doing the wrong thing. You get your own butt in it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was on the carpet a lot of times, just over being, trying to be a favor of men, you know, working. But I was a favor of the men working and I was still standing between the company and the men working. And you can get by with it a little bit and the first thing, you know, I was on the carpet, actually explain why this was done.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because they'd always get in. They'd always get this hall with somebody and carry it to headquarters.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Who was the guy you had to do your explaining to in management?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, the manager. The one that was the top boss, you see. He's the superintendent or the manager.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: For a long time we had Laird in Potlatte. He was the head man for Potlatte's lumber company up to where he died in 30's. Then after that, you know, after he died, they merged Potlatte's lumber, Clearwater Timber and the Rutlett's Timber Company. Made the Potlatte's Forest out of the tree. Then the headquarters was at Lewiston. So all the orders come from Lewiston then after that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the others had a kind of assistant manager, you know, one or two. One was for the woods and one was for the mill crew and then the big boss, you know, he sat in the office. Or maybe in first school, I don't know where. But then they'd come and eat on you, on us, the fella out in the field.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any things that made him real mad? I mean, things that got them the angriest? Things that they called you on the most? The company?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, mostly by all the rages.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: P -shacks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Putting it all in. Not the work, but just me trying to pacify workers, you know. I liked them and they were good workers and sometimes I felt that they didn't get quite enough so I was trying to patch them on the back a little bit. I hit the carpet. Ever so often that happened. That was put in there always. Just over paying somebody. Just slipping. You know, I felt, you know, a ten dollar bill looked pretty nice to a young fella. He's trying his best and I liked him. And I'd, I'd, whatever stealing from the company, sure I knew it. And I paid for it. So I'd get on the carpet every so often.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Why in the hell did they do that? Why?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sometimes, almost, well anyway I guess I got by very cause I stayed with them. I didn't get fired. And I quit. I wasn't let off either. I quit.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In 51.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I even quit six months before I left to work. I was going home. My wife was in Oregon. And we bought a little place there cause I figured when I got old enough by God I wanted to get just as far away from Boville and Elk River as I possibly could. So I went 600 miles out of there. I knew everybody for that whole circle from Moscow to Lewiston to Orphino, Cardellini, St. Mary's. That whole circle. Inside of that I think I knew every man that was in there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In that circle.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And hit the town, you know, the worst of it, a lot of it was, you know, let's go and have one which we did. Well I had to buy one too.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Have a beer. Have a drink.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And go up to the temple or some place, you know that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And if I got into the temple in Cardellini, Elk Temple, I was an elk. And if I got in the temple, I knew half a million was in there. Got into the temple in St. Mary's, you know, the same thing. Go to Orphino, it's the same thing. Go down to Lewiston, the same thing. Given as far as Missoula, I got over there and I knew half a million was in there. They got a lot of all there before I got to Barroom. And that way, you know, and I was on the road the last ten years, I was on the road most of the time. So you meet a lot of people and by God what little, that little paycheck I got, you know, by God I took that in drinks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You can't always take without you got to give too. And most of the time I usually give.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I often thought about Harry Lauder's little song. He said when I was 21, he said I never had much money but he said I always had a lot of fun. And that little piece all went on in my cup. I guess that was just like me. I never had any money but I guess I had a lot of fun.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because after all you can't take it. I never took the job with me home. And if I got to town I never logged. I never talked about it. I'd hate to say it. Only just what little I had to do just to get to because when I went to town, you know, I usually went in there to pick up a few men and take them to work. If I headed up towards Avery there was a camp up there, you know, okay, I got in St. Mary's. Looking on, I always drove a sedan so I could pick up four or five and take them in the car and take them up to camp and put them to work. I came back towards Boville and over there I came back towards St. Mary's and headed for Clarkie. There was another camp and I'd look all the way and we'd usually when I'd drive up to camp there I had a car full and I'd say do you need to mark whoever was camp for them. And there was four or five of them and there was a couple of saw gangs. Had a good cab driver or a choking man or something. You were short, here they are. There were five of them. I mean, okay. Got up towards Alcofford, there was another camp and I'd pick him along the road, drive him and take him up there. Buy him a beer or something, I went through Alcofford.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You're thirsty, alright, let's go in and have a study. I was 40. Art Henderson was in it. He was short, no help during the war. So I was riding with Joe then I left my car in Boville in Butler area in Clarkie and got in with him and we drove into camp.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And he says, what the hell would we do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We said to get to Mary's. I said, the only thing we can do is go to Mary's and see if we can find anybody. It's after supper in the evening. Well, he said, when we got to St.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Mary's, he said, what we'll do now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, let's go to the temple. I said, okay. So we had a snort there, him and I. So I said, well, we come down here to see if we could pick up some saw huts and men to send up to art.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but he said, what are we getting?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, you got to get out in the barrooms, go in the tavern, go down there on the boathouses, any place. Any place but, I said, anybody comes in here to the temple, they're like you and me. They go in here to have a drink, but I said, the lumberjacks don't come in here. They don't belong here. I said, they go in the tavern. That's where they belong and that's where they are. So you got to go to them. They won't come to you.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When he said, do I have to do it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, you have to do it. If you want to get any men, you got to go that way.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But, of course, he said, will you do it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, I'm doing it every day, Joe. That's what I'm doing every day. I said, I'm going all over that. I said, in the first whatever time I get to, I said, that's what I'm doing right now. But I said, I don't like to, but I said, I'll have to. That's my job. And so, as you go ahead, I said, all right, Joe, but I said, you know, I may come back here drunk in a hoot -hock, but I said, I'll try it. So I took off and left them in the temple.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I come back and oh, long about midnight, I guess when they start to close up the barrooms, you know, the taverns. And in the meantime, you know, he said, I was pretty well, I was, oh, I had a lot of beers. But he was in worse shape than I was. He'd stay there at the bar in the temple all evening. He said, oh, they make it out. And I told him, I got so many, three cars that went out of here loaded. I said, there's five in each car. There were 15. And I said, there's that many more going in the morning. So I said, I done pretty well. But I said, it costs like hell to do that. I had to buy a hell of a lot of beer. God damn it, he said, and we got to do that. So then, after that, you know, by God, I had to drive to Bovil. I drove his car. I drove him home. Well, that's too bad. A pot lot should be paying your beer money when you do that kind of work. Well, it was part of the job.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Why did you say that you hated to do that?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you say that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, I could have said that, but I didn't. Because I liked the job. I was satisfied. I meant the going around to the bar. Well, that was part of it. You know, when you're trying to help someone, and you know that's the only way. I was helping them, trying to help that foreman to give him the help he needed. Well, if I hadn't done it, he wouldn't have got them. I was helping him. I wasn't helping myself any.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But, you know, they asked me, they said, for Christ's sake, try and see if you can't get somebody. I drove into Spokane time and time again, you know. I took him out, filled the car up, and take him back to camp. Took him over to Missoula, Montana, took him up to Avery's. Had a car full when I come back with men. Or Fino the same way. Down to Lewiston, down over to Moscow.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you know people in these towns? Were the people you find people that you knew?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, hell yes.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Yes, you know them lumbers?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, they were scattered around all them towns. When they were quit, you know, some of them went down to Lewiston. Some of them went to Spokane. Some of them went to St. Mary's and blow in. And just about the time they got broken, they were ready for the work again. Sure. Hell yes, I knew all of them. They probably were waiting around town for you to come and get them. They didn't come with me anyway, even if they didn't come with me. Or maybe a meal. I don't know. Just whatever happened. Lots of them probably hadn't had any breakfast, and I said, okay, come on over. I haven't either. Let's go and eat.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I couldn't turn in any beer money. But I could turn in the meal.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were a little bit more

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Diamantris, you know. It was easier. He worked with the Swedes there. And I think they had a section boss there. I think he was the one that put her on. Call them dynamites and see the Diamantris.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had an Italian one time. His name was Millefante Andrea. So I called him Andrew. I called him Andrew. Millefante Andrew. Andrew is what I called him all the time. He went back to Italy in World War I. Never a lot of them. Not only my crew went. They were called back from the old country.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Hell of a lot of them. Austrian, and Serbians, and Italians, German, French.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Most of them never came back, did they?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The people from the old country did. Most of the time they were killed, I think. Because they killed them by the millions, you know, in the First World War and Second World War, too.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: They were called back to fight?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Called them back. I asked them to come back and they did. They were not citizens here, you know. Been here two or three years. So they went back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had a bunch there, you know, after the World War. They worked on the section. They just come up to six dollars a day for the section here in the twenties. One old man, you know, he had figured and figured and figured. And finally, by God, he told me one day, he said, I got to go. He said, I'm going home. I'm going back to Austria. He said, I got some money. He figured out he had so many dollars and he got so many, whatever they call it, against the dollar. In Sweden, they're kroner. But I don't know what they call it in some country, Illyries, in England, they did something different again. And in France, you know, they had the francs. When I was in France, you know, we got five francs for a dollar. And in Sweden now, it's five kronens for a dollar. Well, he had stepped into the World War, World War I there, you know. That was up in the forties. And he figured out how much money, six crowns, you know, would make him. He made between 150 and 200 crowns, whatever it was over there. And he figured out he had enough money so he could back his son up a kind of a big farm. Good size farm, you know. We had nothing when he left there and come back. So he said, I can't take it no more. He says, I gotta go home. He took up money and by God, he left it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I know a Swede there after that. In the twenties. He sold over there what he had down on Spokane. And he stayed in Sweden for three, four years and he cashed in again. And it went the other way. And he was there three, four years and he come back with more money than he had when he left.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Never worked. Just had a good time. Had a good visit at home. And he just did the exchange of money, you see. In the money exchange. When he went over, he got so many crowns, you know, for the dollar. And when he come back, you know, just after the war there, when he come back again, the price of the dollar went up and it took just a few crowns to buy the dollar back. So he had more money when he come back than he had when he left.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In dollars.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There's one thing I've been wanting to ask you about potlatch. And that is, from this kind of stuff you say about the money and the salaries being low and them getting mad at you about men giving them money.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that where they tried to make their profit? Was it by trying to give as little salary as they could?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, that was one way.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was that a big deal with them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, yes, of course it is.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You know, if you can get a man to work like for two dollars a day and you have eight hundred in the woods, that's quite a few dollars isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That two dollars a day and you turn it, it'll be sixteen hundred dollars you're paying out each day and just in wages. Now that's one way, yes. And then the lumber, just the same if you can get the price goes up and down like it does now. Now it's up to a goddamn party. Nobody knows. I don't think even the higher up knows really what it is from one day to the next one. Wages up, lumber is out of sight.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did they give you many, many rules to follow? Were there many things they told you to do or not to do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They gave me one there one time. They just managed to come up and he said split the nickel when you let a japeau jump. I'm getting down pretty close and I'm going to go down the pennies.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That was quite a jolt wasn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said you've got to split the nickel. If you can get it for two and a half dollars, don't give him that nickel. Don't give him five or you can get it for two and a half.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sent.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That's pretty tough for the foreman to work against isn't it?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you set the wages for the japeau workers?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, the camp foreman did, yeah.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's a place where I've heard guys say that they would really have disagreements. They'd say they wouldn't see the same way on a skating chance. Sure thing, sure thing. Certainly you got arguments there, you know, each one. But most of the time you had to tell them that that's it. Now you can either take it or leave it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It's up to you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You can't stay there and argue all day. You've got too many places to go. You can't stand friends and spend all your time with one man. You said you'd better take it or leave it. That's all you want to get anyway. Now it's up to you whether you want to take it or not. If you want to go to work, okay, there it is. You get so much. If you don't want to, well, okay, say so and I'll try somebody else.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You can't stay there and argue all day about it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now like we saw, usually at the set rate they were a dollar and twenty cents more. Some years there and they never changed that. So that was either they go fall in timber very early or they don't. That's all there was to it. When they set it from the main office, you know, then you can't change it. I did a few times and I, of course, I got on the carpet, tried to do something that I shouldn't. I said, okay, go to work. I'd like to see you go to work. Take it. Not so much. I'll do so and so on the side of it to help you out. And then you go to work. The first thing you know, you just hear this old actually had to get on the carpet and explain why the hell that was done. So the only thing that I could say was maybe I tried a little too hard to get the job going.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they didn't fire me. I wonder if they knew what you did for them. I wonder if they really understood what a guy on the ground had to do.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had one remark there one time, you know, Billings was the manager at the time and he rode with me all day in the pickup. When we come over, we go back after dinner. In the middle of the afternoon, they were going back to Lewiston. And he said, actually, he said something I want to ask you.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: He said, don't we ever get tired of looking at those old faces?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I don't know, Mr. Billings. I said, I don't know if I can answer that or not, but I said, I'll try. I said, I'll tell you. I said, you put your foot in my shoe and come out here at four o 'clock in the morning. I said, that's when you go to work. First crew goes to work, loading crews go to work at four. And I said, I'll go out with them and see that they're all set. Go to each one of them loading rigs. I got four of them scattered out. I go to each one of them and I said, I'll come back again. And we have breakfast, they have two breakfasts, one at four in the morning and one at six. So I'm back at camp again for breakfast and I said, come out after breakfast. I get out here where you and I are standing now and I hear hearsay. Two or three saw gangs come and I said, they want to know what they were doing on the strip they were on. They want a place to go. And I tell them, they go to strip zones, so they're all fixed. Go and start in there. Walk on, I call them spurs, no roads. We had stock roads scattered out. I said, you go down there and find that number and start on, skidways in and start following them across there and go to work. And I said, I'll be with you sometime today. If I don't get there today, I'll go tomorrow and we'll talk a little more about it. But I said, go on and go to work. Here comes another one, I tell them the same thing. And here comes a teamster, two or three, they're done. And I tell them the same thing, go on over and strip, find so and so and skidways in. All you got to do is go to work. And I said, I'll see you in a day sometime. So I tell them in about 15 minutes, they're all gone, me and the born boys and the black sweatshirts, the only three left. Now I said, if I had to get a new crew all the way, if I didn't have them old faces, I'd have to go with each one of them. Take each one of them out and the rest of them, we had to stay here and wait until I come back. They'd take all day before they got to work and I said, that was this way, the way I feel about it, the way I'm trying it. In 15 minutes, they're all gone and I said, then I'll come and look, I'll go and look them up after that. And we talk about it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Well, he said, I was just wondering, he said, how you, how you done it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, you always run the biggest crew of any of our foremen. And he said, you got the smallest turnover. Internal, we're quitting them. I said, that's my way of doing it. And I said, I know them. And I said, I believe in them. And I said, I hope at least that they're believing in me. Of course, I did take my word for it and go to work. You know, don't lose any time. They'll go back and go to work the next morning again. Well, he said, I often wonder how you did it. And I said, that's it. He was a kick in the butt or a button in the cap. I said, I don't know. That's the way it is with me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And sometime, I guess I tried a little too freely and went a little too far, you know, trying to hold the crew. That I admit. Because I tried to hold, I tried to hold the crew when I got them. If they were, if they played fair with me, I done every darn thing that I possibly could to hold them. I want them to stay. I didn't want to be in the crew. I didn't want to fire anybody.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: If they could get along with me, I tried to get along with them the best I knew how.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You tried to, you tried to see that their work kept going for them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, they would keep going.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Last time they come and they ask me, they said, why don't you come up and see us sometime?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I, I'll tell you enough of you. I said, I'm watching this kid. I said, as long as the logs are coming into that landing, well, I said, I got nothing to talk to you about. I said, you go right ahead. But I said, you slow up a little bit. And I said, I'll be up and see you. See what's wrong. So don't worry about me. I said, I'm worrying about you. And I'm watching you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I'm worried about you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
